content: Healthy aging interventions target multiple levels: (1) Individual level—physical activity (strength/balance training preventing falls), nutrition (adequate protein, vitamin D), cognitive stimulation, social engagement, preventive services (vaccinations, cancer screening with individualized decisions), medication optimization (deprescribing); (2) Clinical models—Geriatric Assessment (comprehensive multidimensional evaluation), Age-Friendly Health Systems (4Ms: What Matters, Medication, Mentation, Mobility), PACE programs for frail elders; (3) Community approaches—senior centers, congregate meals, transportation services, friendly visitor programs, fall prevention classes (Tai Chi, Otago); (4) Environmental modifications—home safety assessments, grab bars, improved lighting, universal design principles; (5) Policy interventions—Social Security, Medicare/Medicaid, Older Americans Act services, caregiver support (respite, training), long-term care financing reform, elder abuse prevention; (6) Technology—remote monitoring, medication dispensers, emergency response systems, telehealth. Person-centered care prioritizes individual goals over disease-focused metrics.
